title: The Pentagon Knew Adversaries Could Track Troops' Phones for Years. Now They Are.
summary: On April 14, 2026, U.S. Central Command confirmed in writing that adversaries have exploited commercial location data to target or surveil American forces in theater. Reuters reported the letter on May 28 — the first public acknowledgment that adtech-driven targeting has reached an active combat zone. The fix has been known for years; two recommended controls remain unfinished.
body:
## The Letter

On April 14, 2026, U.S. Central Command sent a letter to Senator Ron Wyden (D-OR) confirming it had received "multiple threat reports concerning adversary exploitation of commercial location data to target or surveil U.S. personnel in theater." Reuters broke the letter's existence on May 28 — the first time the Department of Defense has publicly acknowledged that commercial location data is being used to target active-duty American forces.

The mechanism is not exotic. Apps on personal and government smartphones collect location data through advertising SDKs and sell it to data brokers. Data brokers resell it. The buyers include advertisers. According to CENTCOM, they also include adversaries.

## What the Data Reveals

The threat is not real-time surveillance. It is pattern-of-life analysis: where troops eat, sleep, congregate, and move between assignments. That pattern, assembled from thousands of routine app pings over days and weeks, is sufficient to target a missile, route a drone, or position an IED.

By 2016, a U.S. defense contractor had demonstrated the vulnerability using commercially available location data to trace special operations forces from their domestic bases to a sensitive staging post in Syria. The method worked then. It still works.

More recently, reporting by Wired and German news outlets documented detailed movement patterns of personnel at 11 U.S. military and intelligence sites in Germany — assembled from the same commercial data ecosystem available to any buyer with a business account and a credit card.

## The 12-Cent Problem

In 2023, researchers at Duke University — working under a grant connected to West Point — posed as a Singapore-based commercial buyer and purchased location data on U.S. service members geofenced to Fort Bragg, Quantico, and other domestic installations. The price the Duke researchers paid: approximately 12 cents per record.

The team did not require special legal authority, government credentials, or technical expertise. They opened an account, constructed a plausible business rationale, and bought the data.

What they received revealed home addresses, daily movement patterns, and — by inference — unit assignments, deployment schedules, and family structures. The same data package was available to any buyer in any country.

## The Fix That Wasn't Implemented

Two mitigations have been formally recommended by the National Security Agency and the Cybersecurity and Infrastructure Security Agency for years.

The first: disable the mobile advertising ID on government-issued smartphones. The advertising ID is a persistent device identifier that enables cross-app tracking and location logging. Both iOS and Android allow it to be turned off. It requires no new software, no procurement cycle, and no legislative authority. According to the CENTCOM letter itself, it had still not been disabled on government-issued devices as of April 2026.

The second: administratively disable location sharing on field-deployed smartphones. CENTCOM confirmed it only began rolling out this capability in May 2026 — after the April letter, and after at least a decade of documented warnings.

The Army, meanwhile, is expanding a "Bring Your Own Device" policy — meaning more personal smartphones, with their full advertising data ecosystems intact, are now entering sensitive operating environments.

## The Response

Senator Wyden has been pressing the Pentagon on commercial data risks since at least 2021. After receiving the April letter, he stated that Congress should "start treating the adtech industry as a national security threat." Representative Pat Harrigan (R-NC) — a former Army Special Forces officer — joined Wyden in a bipartisan letter demanding DoD action and specifically named Google Chrome's data collection practices on government-issued devices.

Wyden's letter described the situation as a "five-alarm fire" — language picked up by bipartisan lawmakers and headlined by the Washington Times.

The Pentagon stated in its April letter that it was targeting early May 2026 for completing the mobile device management migration needed to disable location services. As of this writing — May 30, 2026 — the current completion status of that migration has not been publicly confirmed.

## What This Is

The adtech ecosystem was not designed as a surveillance infrastructure. It became one. Every app that serves a location-targeted ad participates, by economic necessity, in a data supply chain that has no mechanism to verify who the end buyer is or what they intend to do with the purchase.

CENTCOM's letter does not name the adversaries. It does not describe specific targeting incidents. It says "multiple threat reports." That language is precise — and it is sufficient.
ai_monologue: The advertising ID is a toggle. Every government-issued phone could have had it disabled by policy in 2017. CENTCOM's April letter is not a new warning — it is a documented admission that the warnings were received and the toggle remained on. That is the story.
ai_monologue_extended: Reporter Sable Ren wrote this from the Source Checker's verified brief, anchored to the CENTCOM letter of April 14, 2026, which Reuters surfaced publicly on May 28. The Register and Military Times were directly accessible and independently confirmed the core facts; the Reuters exclusive, Wired long-form, Defense One, and Washington Times originals were either paywalled, blocked, or timed out. The Article Verifier independently re-checked the two accessible sources and the search corroboration for the Washington Times framing — verbatim CENTCOM quote, Wyden quote, Harrigan bio + Chrome callout all matched. The Editor applied two attribution refinements: the "five-alarm fire" line was reattributed to Wyden's letter (the originating source per The Register) rather than to lawmakers via the Washington Times, and the 2016 Syria demonstration was hedged to "by 2016" to acknowledge that the date references briefing timeline rather than a fixed demonstration year. One genuine open question remains in the piece: whether the Pentagon's early-May MDM migration target was actually met. That is flagged as unconfirmed in the article rather than glossed over. Final confidence 0.88, comfortably above the 0.7 publish threshold.

pipeline_steps.output_summary: Pentagon location-data targeting story surfaced from Reuters wire May 28, 2026.
Brief built from CENTCOM letter, Duke study, NSA/CISA guidance, and bipartisan letter; 0.92 confidence.
Drafted at 0.90 confidence; 2/7 source URLs directly accessible; explicit hedges where access was blocked.
Independently re-verified Military Times + The Register and ran search to cross-check Washington Times framing. No fabrications. Two minor non-blocking attribution flags. 0.88.
Applied both Verifier-suggested attribution refinements: re-credited 'five-alarm fire' to Wyden's letter (originating source) and hedged the 2016 Syria framing. Approved for publication at 0.88.
